# The Hidden Cost of Resampling: How Imbalance Correction Degrades Probability Calibration in Tree Ensembles and Neural Networks Zewen Liu Qilu Institute of Technology, School of Software Engineering Jinan, China lzw7071@gmail.com June 28, 2026 ## **Abstract** Resampling methods such as SMOTE and random under/over-sampling are standard tools for classimbalanced classification, almost always evaluated by minority-class accuracy or F1. Prior work has established that undersampling degrades probability calibration by distorting the training prior Dal Pozzolo et al. (2015). We extend this lens to _synthetic oversampling_ (SMOTE) and provide a practical, evidence-based guide to when calibration damage matters and how to fix it. Across five public datasets (imbalance ratio 1.9–70), two ensemble models and a multi-layer perceptron (random forest, gradient boosting, MLP), with ten seeds and paired statistics, we find: (1) SMOTE’s calibration cost is real but small (ECE +0 _._ 009; Cliff’s _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27, small-tomoderate) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the calibration penalty; (2) random undersampling is the genuine danger—its damage grows sharply with imbalance, inflating ECE from 0 _._ 008 to 0 _._ 395 on a dataset with ratio 70, largely because the resulting training sets are too small to estimate probabilities reliably; (3) a single post-hoc recalibration step (Platt or isotonic) eliminates the damage, reducing ECE by up to 66% at a negligible ranking-power cost (AUC _−_ 0 _._ 002, Cliff’s _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 07); and (4) the analytic prior-shift correction that repairs undersampling does _not_ transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior—so data-driven recalibration remains necessary. A multi-layer perceptron baseline replicates the core calibration findings (ECE 0 _._ 056 _→_ 0 _._ 088 under SMOTE, 0 _._ 225 under RUS, repaired to 0 _._ 025 by Platt scaling), confirming that the pattern is not specific to tree ensembles. We recommend that imbalanced-learning studies report calibration alongside discrimination, and that practitioners recalibrate after resampling whenever predicted probabilities drive decisions. ## **1. Introduction** Class imbalance is pervasive in high-stakes classification: fraud detection, disease screening, and credit default all present far fewer positive than negative cases. The dominant remedy is _resampling_ — oversampling the minority class (e.g., SMOTE Chawla et al. (2002)), undersampling the majority, or both. A large literature has grown around these methods Fern´andez et al. (2018); He and Garcia (2009); Haixiang et al. (2017), and they are routinely judged by how much they improve minority-class recall, F1, or AUC. **Motivating example: cost-sensitive fraud detection.** Consider a credit-card fraud detector whose output is a probability _p_ ˆ(fraud _|_ transaction). The operations team does not act on a hard label; they sort transactions by descending probability and investigate the top _k_ , where _k_ is determined by staffing. If the model is miscalibrated— assigning, say, probability 0.3 to transactions whose true fraud rate is only 0.1—the team wastes two-thirds of its investigation budget on false leads. Calibration error in this setting translates directly to operational cost. Yet standard imbalanced-learning studies report only F1 and AUC, leaving this cost invisible. This evaluation lens is incomplete. Many deployed systems consume a _predicted probability_ that feeds an expected-cost decision, a triage threshold, or a downstream risk model Elkan (2001). For such systems, the quality that matters is _calibration_ : among cases assigned probability _p_ , roughly a fraction _p_ should be positive Guo et al. (2017). Resampling deliberately distorts the class prior the model sees during training, so it is reasonable to suspect it also distorts the probabilities the model emits— yet calibration is rarely measured in imbalanced-learning studies Huang et al. (2020). We ask three questions and answer them empirically on real public data: **(H1)** Does resampling degrade the calibration of tree ensembles and a neural network? **(H2)** Can 1 a cheap post-hoc recalibration repair the damage without sacrificing ranking power? **(H3)** How does the damage scale with the imbalance ratio, and what drives it? Dal Pozzolo et al. Dal Pozzolo et al. (2015) established that random undersampling harms calibration by distorting the training prior and exploited this structure with a closed-form correction. We extend this lens to _synthetic oversampling_ (SMOTE) and provide the first unified, statistically rigorous comparison that places SMOTE, random over/under-sampling, and a class-weight control on the same footing across five datasets, three model families (two tree ensembles and a multi-layer perceptron), ten seeds, and multiple repair strategies. Our contributions are: - C1. **A reproducible evaluation framework.** A multiseed, paired-statistics protocol (5 datasets, 3 model families, 10 seeds) measuring both calibration (ECE, Brier, reliability diagrams) and discrimination (AUC, PR-AUC, F1), with Cliff’s _δ_ effect sizes and Holm– Bonferroni correction (Sections 5.2–5.4). - C2. **Empirical calibration audit.** SMOTE’s calibration cost is real but small (ECE +0 _._ 009, _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the penalty; random undersampling is the genuine danger—its damage grows steeply with imbalance (ECE 0 _._ 008 _→_ 0 _._ 395 on IR 70). A balanceddownsample control reveals that at extreme imbalance the dominant mechanism is training-set collapse ( _∼_ 50 samples), not undersampling _per se_ (Section 5.4). - C3. **Oversampling-ratio sweep.** Calibration error increases monotonically with SMOTE aggressiveness ( _ρ_ from 0 to 1 _._ 0), while AUC and F1 remain flat— so the cost is invisible to standard evaluation metrics (Section 5.4). - C4. **A negative result with mechanistic explanation.** The analytic prior-shift correction that repairs undersampling does _not_ transfer to SMOTE ( _p_ =0 _._ 80, no improvement), because SMOTE distorts the classconditional density _p_ ( **x** _| y_ ) rather than only the prior—so a data-driven recalibration step (Platt or isotonic) remains necessary for synthetic oversampling (Section 6). - C5. **Practical decision guide.** Four actionable recommendations distilled from the full analysis, including when to prefer class weighting over resampling and when to add a post-hoc calibration stage (Section 7.1). All data, code, and the full 700-row results table are released for reproducibility. ## **2. Related Work** **Imbalanced learning.** SMOTE Chawla et al. (2002) introduced synthetic minority interpolation, generating new positive examples along line segments between minority neighbours rather than duplicating them. The idea spawned a large family—Borderline-SMOTE Han et al. (2005), ADASYN He et al. (2008), safe-level, and ensemble-integrated variants among them—surveyed in several retrospectives Fern´andez et al. (2018); He and Garcia (2009); Haixiang et al. (2017); Leevy et al. (2018). Random over- and under-sampling remain strong, simple baselines that the same surveys report are often competitive with elaborate synthesis. Across this literature the evaluation protocol is remarkably uniform: methods are ranked by minority-sensitive discrimination scores (F1, G-mean, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC), and the predicted probability is treated as an intermediate quantity to be thresholded, not an output whose quality is assessed in its own right. Our work does not propose a new sampler; it reexamines this entire family through a metric the family has overlooked. **Tree ensembles.** Random forests Breiman (2001) and gradient boosting Chen and Guestrin (2016) are the default models for tabular data, and they calibrate very differently out of the box: bagging tends to produce probabilities pushed away from 0 and 1, while boosting produces sharp, overconfident scores. Because these two mechanisms react differently to a shifted training prior, we study both rather than a single model. **Calibration.** A probabilistic classifier is calibrated when, among instances assigned probability _p_ , a fraction _p_ are positive. Post-hoc methods learn a mapping from raw scores to calibrated probabilities on heldout data: Platt scaling Platt (1999) fits a sigmoid; isotonic regression fits a non-parametric monotone function Niculescu-Mizil and Caruana (2005); Zadrozny and Elkan (2001); and beta calibration Kull et al. (2017) offers a richer parametric family. Miscalibration is not unique to imbalance—modern deep networks are systematically overconfident Guo et al. (2017); Minderer et al. (2021), and calibration can degrade further under distribution shift Ovadia et al. (2019). The choice of ECE estimator also matters: equal-width binning is known to be biased with finite samples, motivating adaptive-binning and KDE-based alternatives Kumar et al. (2019); Nixon et al. (2019). We use equal-width ECE as our primary metric for comparability with prior work but mitigate its limitations through complementary Brier scores and reliability diagrams, and verify robustness across four binning strategies (Appendix 9). The interaction between _deliberate prior distortion via resampling_ and calibration has not been measured systematically at this breadth. That interaction is our subject. **Closest prior work.** Two studies are directly related. Dal Pozzolo et al. Dal Pozzolo et al. (2015) established that random undersampling distorts posterior probabilities via a training-prior shift and derived a closed-form correction. Huang et al. Huang et al. (2020) experimentally compared several calibration techniques on imbalanced data. Table 1 positions our study relative to these two works across design dimensions that matter for the calibration assessment. Our contribution extends the lens to _synthetic oversampling_ (SMOTE) under a unified multiseed, paired-statistics protocol, adds an oversamplingratio sweep and a dedicated class-weight control, and reports a _negative result_ (Section 6) with a mechanistic explanation: the prior-only correction that repairs undersampling fails for SMOTE because SMOTE alters the classconditional density rather than only the prior. ## **3. Method** ## **3.1. Calibration metrics** Let _p_ ˆ _i_ be the predicted probability of the positive class for test instance _i_ with true label _yi ∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ . We report three complementary measures. **Expected Calibration Error (ECE)** partitions [0 _,_ 1] into _M_ = 10 equal-width bins _Bm_ and computes **==> picture [206 x 30] intentionally omitted <==** where acc( _Bm_ ) is the empirical positive rate and conf( _Bm_ ) the mean predicted probability in bin _m_ . The **Brier score** is the mean squared error _n_[1] � _i_[(ˆ] _[p][i][−][y][i]_[)][2][.] We visualise calibration with **reliability diagrams** . To confirm that any calibration change is not bought by destroying ranking ability, we also report ROC-AUC and PR-AUC, and—to make the “hidden cost” explicit— minority-class F1. ## **3.2. Conditions** Against an unsampled **baseline** , we evaluate three resampling families applied _inside the training fold only_ : SMOTE, random oversampling (ROS), and random undersampling (RUS). To separate “the resampling” from “the model’s behaviour under imbalance,” we add a **classweight** control that rebalances the loss without altering the data (scikit-learn’s class ~~w~~ eight=’balanced’ for random forest and class ~~w~~ eight=’balanced’ for histogram gradient boosting, which weight samples inversely proportional to class frequency). Finally we apply two **post-hoc recalibrations** to the SMOTE model: Platt scaling (sigmoid) and isotonic regression, each fit on a held-out calibration split disjoint from the model’s training data. ## **4. Experimental Setup** ## **4.1. Datasets** We use five real public binary-classification datasets from OpenML, chosen to span a wide imbalance ratio (IR, majority:minority), shown in Table 2. Categorical features are one-hot encoded and missing values imputed by column medians. The large adult set is stratifiedsubsampled to 8,000 rows to bound compute. ## **4.2. Protocol** Each (dataset, model, condition) cell is evaluated with 5- fold stratified cross-validation repeated over 10 random seeds; resampling and calibration-split selection occur strictly within training folds to prevent leakage. Models are scikit-learn’s random forest (120 trees) and histogram gradient boosting (200 iterations). We aggregate the outof-fold predictions per seed and compare conditions with the paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on matched (dataset, model, seed) tuples, report Cliff’s _δ_ as a non-parametric effect size, and apply Holm–Bonferroni correction across the primary ECE comparisons. All code, data identifiers, and seeds are released for reproducibility. ## **4.3. Statistical methodology** Because metric values across datasets are neither normally distributed nor on a common scale, we avoid parametric tests. For each comparison we form matched pairs over the 5 _×_ 2 _×_ 10 = 100 (dataset, model, seed) tuples— reduced to 50 when a comparison fixes the model—and apply the two-sided _Wilcoxon signed-rank test_ , which assesses whether the median paired difference departs from zero without assuming normality. To quantify _how large_ a difference is, independent of sample size, we report _Cliff’s[j]_[)][ :] _[ a][i][> b][j][}][ −]_[#] _[{]_[(] _[i][,][j]_[)][ :] _[ a][i][< b][j][}] δ_ =[#] _[{]_[(] _[i][,]_ , the prob- _na nb_ ability that a random draw from one condition exceeds a random draw from the other. For each comparison we compute _δ_ on the paired differences (i.e., we form _di_ = _ai−bi_ for each matched tuple and compute _δ_ on the resulting delta distribution), which preserves the paired structure and yields a more familiar scale than rank-biserial correlation for readers of the imbalanced-learning literature (by convention _|δ| <_ 0 _._ 15 negligible, _<_ 0 _._ 33 small, _<_ 0 _._ 47 medium, larger values large). Reporting _δ_ alongside _p_ is what lets us state honestly that the AUC cost of calibration, though statistically significant, is negligible in effect. Finally, because H1 and H2 together pose five primary ECE comparisons, we control the family-wise error rate with the _Holm–Bonferroni_ step-down procedure; all five survive correction. **Table 1:** Positioning relative to the two closest prior studies. |Design dimension|Dal Pozzolo|Huang et al.|This work| |---|---|---|---| ||(2015)|(2020)|| |Undersampling|✓|✓|✓| |SMOTE (synthetic oversampling)|—|✓|✓| |Random oversampling|—|—|✓| |Class-weight control|—|—|✓| |Platt & isotonic recalibration|—|✓|✓| |Prior-correction on SMOTE|—|—|✓(negative)| |_ρ_-sweep (oversampling ratio)|—|—|✓| |Datasets (imbalance range)|1|several|5 (1.9–70)| |Multi-seed repetition|—|—|10 seeds| |Paired Wilcoxon + effect size (_δ_)|—|—|✓| |Holm–Bonferroni correction|—|—|✓| |SHAP attribution analysis|—|—|✓| |Balanced-downsample control|—|—|✓| |Learning-curve control|—|—|✓| |Full data, code, results released|—|—|✓| |Neural network baseline|—|—|✓| **Table 2:** Datasets (after preprocessing). |Dataset|_n_|Features|IR|Minority %| |---|---|---|---|---| |pima

The pattern is consistent: each resampling family raises ECE relative to the baseline while leaving (or slightly improving) F1 and AUC—precisely the metrics practitioners watch—so the calibration cost is invisible to standard evaluation. ## **5.2. H3: Damage scales with imbalance** Stratifying by dataset imbalance ratio (Fig. 5) reveals that undersampling’s harm grows steeply with IR: on yeast ~~m~~ l8 (IR= 70), ECE inflates from 0.008 to 0.395 ( _δ_ = +0 _._ 39), whereas SMOTE’s inflation stays below 0.02 across the whole IR range. **However, a control experiment disentangles the causes.** We trained the same RF model on a balanced random subsample of yeast ~~m~~ l8 containing exactly as many training points as RUS produces ( _∼_ 50). On datasets where RUS leaves adequate training data (e.g., adult, _∼_ 18,700 train samples), RUS ECE rises to 0 _._ 123 vs. baseline 0 _._ 022—a genuine resampling effect. The practical message is: RUS is dangerous both because it discards data _and_ because it distorts the training prior; on extreme imbalances the former dominates. The per-dataset view (Table 4) confirms the aggregate is not an artefact of averaging: ECE rises under SMOTE on all five datasets and under undersampling on all five, while isotonic recalibration yields the lowest ECE on every dataset, including the extreme yeast ~~m~~ l8. ## **5.5. Discrimination is preserved, and the cost stays hidden** Table 3 makes the central irony quantitative. Minorityclass F1 actually _rises_ under every resampling family (0 _._ 530 _→_ 0 _._ 546 SMOTE, 0 _._ 555 RUS), and ROC-AUC moves by less than 0 _._ 02; PR-AUC is likewise flat (within 0 _._ 03). A practitioner tuning on any of these three metrics would conclude resampling helped, while ECE tells the opposite story—this is precisely why the cost is hid- den. Notably, the calibration repair lowers F1 slightly ( _→_ 0 _._ 508): post-hoc calibration optimises probability quality, not the 0 _._ 5-threshold label, so the two objectives can diverge. Reporting both is therefore essential. ## **5.6. The two ensembles start from different calibration** Averaged over conditions, the random forest is far better calibrated than histogram gradient boosting at baseline (ECE 0 _._ 023 vs 0 _._ 082), consistent with the known tendency of boosting to produce sharp, overconfident scores. Crucially, resampling degrades _both_ : SMOTE raises random-forest ECE from 0 _._ 023 to 0 _._ 033 and boosting ECE from 0 _._ 082 to 0 _._ 089. The harm is therefore not an artefact of one model’s idiosyncratic probability behaviour; it appears in the well-calibrated bagging model and the poorly-calibrated boosting model alike, which strengthens the case that the cause is the shifted training prior rather than the estimator. **A learning-curve control confirms these are not convergence artefacts.** Varying _n_ estimators from 5 to 300 trees (RF) and 10 to 500 iterations (HGB) across five seeds and all five datasets, we find: RF calibration _improves_ with more trees (baseline ECE 0 _._ 062 _→_ 0 _._ 024; SMOTE 0 _._ 078 _→_ 0 _._ 035), while HGB calibration _worsens_ (baseline ECE 0 _._ 061 _→_ 0 _._ 125; SMOTE 0 _._ 133 _→_ 0 _._ 121). Boosting’s poor calibration is inherent to the algorithm, not an underfitting issue—and resampling compounds it regardless of _n_ estimators. ## **5.7. Calibrating Without Resampling: The Best Achievable Calibration** A natural follow-up question is whether calibrating the baseline and class-weight models—without any resampling—achieves better calibration than the SMOTEcalibrated pipeline. We add four conditions: Baseline+Platt, Baseline+Isotonic, ClassWeight+Platt, and ClassWeight+Isotonic, each using the same 70-30 calibration split (Table 3). The results are instructive. Calibrating the baseline directly yields ECE 0 _._ 052 _→_ 0 _._ 022 (Isotonic), which is _comparable to or better than_ the SMOTE-calibrated pipeline (ECE 0 _._ 025, SMOTE+Isotonic). Calibrating the class-weight model similarly reaches ECE 0 _._ 025. In absolute terms, the best calibration is achieved without resampling. However, the trade-off remains: SMOTE lifts AUC from 0 _._ 850 to 0 _._ 867 and minority-class F1 from 0 _._ 530 to 0 _._ 546, while the calibrated baseline actually _loses_ AUC (Baseline+Isotonic AUC 0 _._ 826, drop _−_ 0 _._ 024) due to the calibration split. A practitioner who values discrimination above all may accept a small calibration penalty for the SMOTE gains and then recalibrate to recover calibration quality. Neural Network Baseline: Generalisation Across Model Families** A critical question is whether the calibration damage from resampling is specific to tree ensembles or a broader phenomenon. We replicate the full experiment with a multilayer perceptron (MLP; two hidden layers of 64 and 32 units, ReLU, early stopping, features standardised foldby-fold) across all five datasets, ten seeds, and the five conditions: baseline, SMOTE, RUS, SMOTE+Platt, and SMOTE+Isotonic (Table 5). The pattern replicates cleanly. MLP baseline calibration (ECE 0 _._ 056) is comparable to the wellcalibrated random forest (0 _._ 023) and far better than boosting (0 _._ 082). SMOTE degrades MLP calibration (ECE 0 _._ 056 _→_ 0 _._ 088, _δ_ =+0 _._ 39, _p<_ 10 _[−]_[2] ) while boosting discrimination (AUC 0 _._ 772 _→_ 0 _._ 836)—the same “hidden cost” pattern observed in tree ensembles. Random undersampling is again the genuine danger: MLP+RUS ECE reaches 0 _._ 225 ( _δ_ =+0 _._ 92, _p<_ 10 _[−]_[3] ), comparable to the tree-ensemble RUS damage (ECE 0 _._ 186, _δ_ =+0 _._ 77). Post-hoc recalibration repairs the damage in MLP as effectively as in trees: Platt reduces ECE from 0 _._ 088 to 0 _._ 025 ( _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 71), and isotonic reduces it to 0 _._ 024 ( _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 82), both below the unmodified MLP baseline. The key conclusion is that resampling-induced calibration degradation is _not_ a tree-ensemble artefact. It appears consistently across random forests, gradient boosting, and neural networks, under both synthetic oversampling and random undersampling. The underlying mechanism—training-prior distortion—replicates across all three model families tested (random forests, gradient boosting, and a shallow MLP), and so is the remedy: a held-out calibration step repairs the damage across all three model families. **==> picture [230 x 159] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

Calibration error increases monotonically with the amount of synthetic minority data for RF and broadly for HGB/MLP; the dashed line is the across-dataset mean. ## **5.9. H3b: Damage grows with oversampling aggressiveness** The imbalance-ratio trend raises a follow-up question: is the harm driven by _how much_ synthetic data we inject? We sweep the SMOTE oversampling ratio _ρ_ from 0 (no oversampling) to 1 _._ 0 (full balance) on all three model families (RF, HGB, MLP; ten seeds each; Fig. 3). The random forest shows strictly monotonic ECE inflation with _ρ_ (0 _._ 023 _→_ 0 _._ 033, ∆= + 0 _._ 010), while AUC and F1 rise concurrently—the hidden-cost pattern again. Gradient boosting exhibits a flatter trajectory: ECE moves only 0 _._ 082 _→_ 0 _._ 089 (∆= + 0 _._ 007), consistent with HGB’s already-poor baseline calibration dominating the signal. The MLP exhibits a non-monotonic trajectory: a small amount of synthetic minority mass initially _improves_ calibration (ECE 0 _._ 056 _→_ 0 _._ 048 at _ρ_ =0 _._ 25) before the damage accumulates and ECE rises to 0 _._ 089 at _ρ_ =1 _._ 0. Across all three families, the overall direction is consistent—more synthetic data worsens calibration at the extremes— but the trajectory shape depends on the model family’s baseline calibration and sensitivity to the training prior. The extreme case makes the mechanism visible. Fig. 4 shows the reliability diagram for yeast ml8: after undersampling rebalances a 70:1 problem to 1:1, the model’s predicted probabilities sit far above the observed frequencies—it has effectively learned the wrong prior and become severely overconfident, which is exactly the 0 _._ 395 ECE reported in Table 4. ## **5.10. Full per-condition breakdown** For completeness, Table 6 reports ECE for all seven conditions on all five datasets. Two regularities hold without exception. What resampling does** _**not**_ **damage: feature attributions** A natural worry is that resampling might also distort _what_ the model learns, not merely the scale of its probabilities. As an initial check on one dataset, we test this with SHAP attributions on credit-g (48 features, gradient boosting), comparing mean _|_ SHAP _|_ feature importances of the baseline and SMOTE models (Fig. 6). On this dataset, the two rankings are almost identical: the Spearman rank correlation between baseline and SMOTE feature importances is 0 _._ 96, and the top features (e.g. checking ~~s~~ tatus) keep their order and roughly their magnitudes. This suggests that—at least for credit-g—resampling leaves the model’s learned feature structure largely intact while corrupting the probabil- **==> picture [230 x 159] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

A Negative Result: Prior Correction Does Not Transfer to SMOTE** For undersampling, the calibration damage is essentially a known prior shift, and Dal Pozzolo et al. Dal Pozzolo et al. (2015) exploit this with a closed-form correction. Because SMOTE balances the training prior to _π_ train=0 _._ 5 while deployment faces the true prior _π_ test, the same analytic correction Saerens et al. (2002); Elkan (2001) is tempting: it requires _no held-out calibration data_ and, being monotone, cannot harm AUC, **==> picture [220 x 21] intentionally omitted <==** We tested this head-to-head against isotonic recalibration on the SMOTE model (Table 7). The result is negative and instructive: the analytic correction does _not_ improve calibration on SMOTE (ECE 0 _._ 090 _→_ 0 _._ 096, Wilcoxon _p_ =0 _._ 80—no significant change), and on adult it is markedly worse (0 _._ 035 _→_ 0 _._ 082). Data-driven isotonic recalibration, by contrast, cuts ECE sharply (0 _._ 090 _→_ 0 _._ 026, _p<_ 10 _[−]_[14] ). As predicted, prior correction leaves AUC untouched (0 _._ 863, identical to uncorrected SMOTE), but that is cold comfort when it fails to fix the calibration it was meant to repair. The explanation is that the correction’s assumption is violated. Prior adjustment is exact only when resampling shifts _p_ ( _y_ ) alone, leaving the class-conditional density _p_ ( **x** _| y_ ) intact—true for random undersampling, but _not_ for SMOTE, which synthesises new minority points by interpolation and thereby alters _p_ ( **x** _| y_ =1) itself. The shift SMOTE induces is therefore not a pure prior shift, and **==> picture [231 x 185] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

The attribution ranking is essentially preserved (Spearman _ρ_ = 0 _._ 96); resampling harms the probability scale, not the learned feature structure. **Table 7:** Analytic prior correction vs. data-driven isotonic on the SMOTE model (gradient boosting, mean over datasets and ten seeds). The practical lesson reinforces our main message: there is no free, data-free shortcut for SMOTE-induced miscalibration; a held-out, data-driven recalibration step is necessary. ## **7. Discussion** Our results reframe a routine modelling choice. Resampling is adopted to help the minority class, and by discrimination metrics it appears to do so—F1 is flat or slightly higher. But the same intervention quietly corrupts the predicted probabilities, and the corruption is largest exactly where imbalance is most severe and resampling is most tempting. A practitioner who selects a pipeline on F1 or AUC alone will ship a model whose probabilities are untrustworthy without ever seeing a warning sign. The remedy is cheap and well understood: recalibrate after resampling. One Platt or isotonic step restored calibration to approximately baseline levels (and below baseline in the full 5-dataset aggregate; Table 3), and because both are monotone they leave the decision ranking intact. Notably, directly calibrating the baseline or class-weight model—without any resampling—achieves the best absolute calibration (Baseline+Isotonic ECE 0 _._ 022; Section 5.7), at the cost of a modest AUC reduction ( _−_ 0 _._ 024) from the held-out calibration split. The practical guidance is therefore: if you resample and your downstream system consumes probabilities, add a held-out recalibration stage, and prefer SMOTE or oversampling over aggressive undersampling when imbalance is extreme. If calibrated probabilities are the primary objective and the dataset is large, calibrating the baseline directly is the simplest path to the lowest ECE. ## **7.1. Practical recommendations** We distil the findings into four concrete guidelines. **(1) Always report calibration.** ECE, Brier, or a reliability diagram should accompany F1/AUC in any imbalancedlearning study; otherwise a real regression in probability quality is invisible. **(2) Recalibrate after resampling** on a held-out split whenever probabilities feed a threshold or expected-cost decision; one isotonic or Platt step suffices and restored ECE to approximately baseline levels (and below baseline in the 5-dataset aggregate) in every case we measured. **(3) Avoid aggressive undersampling at high imbalance.** Its calibration damage is an order of magnitude larger than SMOTE’s and grows with the imbalance ratio; if data volume permits, prefer oversampling or class weighting. **(4) Prefer class weighting when a calibrated probability is the goal and the model supports it** , since it barely perturbed calibration (ECE 0 _._ 052 _→_ 0 _._ 058) while still addressing the loss imbalance. **(5) Calibrate even without resampling:** calibrating the baseline or class-weight model directly yields the lowest absolute ECE in our experiments (Baseline+Isotonic ECE 0 _._ 022; Section 5.7), making it the simplest path to high-quality probabilities when discrimination gains from resampling are not essential. ## **7.2. Reproducibility** All datasets are public OpenML sets identified by numeric ID; the preprocessing (one-hot encoding, median imputation, stratified subsampling of adult to 8,000 rows) is deterministic given the seed. Every reported number is the mean over ten seeds and 5-fold stratified cross-validation, with resampling and calibration-split selection confined to training folds. We release the data-fetch script, the experiment runner, the analysis script, and the figure generator, together with the full 700-row results table, so that every figure and statistic can be regenerated end to end. ## **8. Limitations** Our scope is deliberately bounded and our claims should not be overstated. (i) We study two tree ensembles and one MLP architecture on tabular data; deeper networks, transformers, and other modalities may behave differently. The MLP architecture tested (two hidden layers of 64 and 32 units) is a reasonable baseline but does not represent the full space of neural architectures. (ii) The calibration–AUC trade-off involves both a data-reduction cost ( _∼_ 42% of the total AUC drop; see Section 5.3) and a calibration-transform cost; varying the calibration split size would further refine this decomposition. (iii) A learning-curve control (evaluated across 5–300 trees for RF, 10–500 iterations for HGB; Section 5.4) confirms RF calibration improves with more trees while HGB calibration worsens, so the reported differences are not underfitting artefacts. (iv) A balanced-downsampling control on yeast ~~m~~ l8 (§5.4) shows the extreme RUS ECE (0 _._ 395) reflects training on _∼_ 50 samples rather than resampling _per se_ ; RUS remains genuinely damaging on datasets with adequate training size. (v) ECE with four binning strategies (Appendix 9) yields consistent conclusions; equal-width-10 slightly underestimates ECE, so our reported numbers are conservative. (vi) SHAP analysis is limited to credit-g. (vii) Five moderate-scale tabular datasets cannot represent every regime, particularly largescale extreme-imbalance settings (e.g., credit card fraud at 0.1% prevalence). (viii) The analytic prior-correction experiment uses known test priors; EM-based label-shift estimation Saerens et al. (2002) is a stronger baseline under unknown deployment priors and remains a natural next step. (ix) We evaluate Platt and isotonic calibration; beta calibration Kull et al. (2017) and alternative SMOTE variants (e.g., Borderline-SMOTE, ADASYN) are left to future work. (x) The interaction between miscalibration and threshold-moving or cost-sensitive decision rules is not studied here; a dedicated analysis would strengthen applied relevance. ## **9. Conclusion** Resampling for class imbalance can degrade probability calibration, but the severity depends on the method: SMOTE’s cost is real but small ( _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the penalty; random undersampling is the genuine danger, especially at high imbalance ratios, where the problem is as much about sample-size collapse as about resampling _per se_ . These patterns replicate across random forests, gradient boosting, and a multi-layer perceptron (Section 5.8), indicating that the underlying mechanism—training-prior distortion—replicates across all three model families. A single post-hoc recalibration step (Platt or isotonic) repairs the damage in all three model families at negligible ranking cost (AUC _−_ 0 _._ 002, Cliff’s _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 07). The analytic prior-shift correction that works for undersampling does not transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior— so data-driven recalibration remains the only reliable re- pair. We urge that imbalanced-learning evaluations report calibration alongside F1 and AUC, and that probabilityconsuming systems recalibrate after any resampling. ## **Appendix: ECE estimator robustness** The primary results use equal-width ECE with 10 bins. To verify conclusions are not an artefact of this estimator, we recomputed ECE with four variants on two datasets (pima, credit ~~g~~ , RF, 10 seeds): equalwidth-10, equal-width-15, adaptive-binning (equal mass per bin), and ECEsweep (mean over 5–30 bins; Roelofs et al.). All agree in sign and rank order (Table 8): RUS _≫_ SMOTE _>_ baseline _>_ calibrated. Equal-width-10 slightly _underestimates_ ECE relative to adaptive/sweep, so our reported numbers are conservative. **Table 8:** ECE across four binning strategies (mean, 2 datasets, RF model, 10 seeds). |Condition|EW-10|EW-15|Adaptive|Sweep| |---|---|---|---|---| |Baseline|0.032|0.035|0.031|0.039| |SMOTE|0.045|0.048|0.046|0.051| |RUS|0.130|0.131|0.130|0.131| |SMOTE+Platt|0.032|0.042|0.037|0.043| ## **References** - Leo Breiman. metadata.title: The Hidden Cost of Resampling: How Imbalance Correction Degrades Probability Calibration in Tree Ensembles and Neural Networks

content_markdown:
# The Hidden Cost of Resampling: How Imbalance Correction Degrades Probability Calibration in Tree Ensembles ## Zewen Liu _Qilu Institute of Technology_ Tai’an, Shandong, China liuzewen@qilu.edu.cn _**Abstract**_ **—Resampling methods such as SMOTE and random under/over-sampling are standard tools for class-imbalanced classification, almost always evaluated by minority-class accuracy or F1. Prior work has established that undersampling degrades probability calibration by distorting the training prior [1]. We extend this lens to** _**synthetic oversampling**_ **(SMOTE) and provide a practical, evidence-based guide to when calibration damage matters and how to fix it. Across five public datasets (imbalance ratio 1.9–70) and two ensemble models (random forest, gradient boosting), with ten seeds and paired statistics, we find: (1) SMOTE’s calibration cost is real but small (ECE** +0 _._ 009 **; Cliff’s** _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27 **, small-to-moderate) across the studied imbalance range (IR 1.9–70) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the calibration penalty; (2) random undersampling is the genuine danger—its damage grows sharply with imbalance, inflating ECE from** 0 _._ 008 **to** 0 _._ 395 **on a dataset with ratio 70, largely because the resulting training sets are too small to estimate probabilities reliably; (3) a single post-hoc recalibration step (Platt or isotonic) eliminates the damage, reducing ECE by up to 66% at a negligible ranking-power cost (AUC** _−_ 0 _._ 002 **, Cliff’s** _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 07 **); and (4) the analytic prior-shift correction that repairs undersampling does** _**not**_ **transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior—so data-driven recalibration remains necessary. We recommend that imbalanced-learning studies report calibration alongside discrimination, and that practitioners recalibrate after resampling whenever predicted probabilities drive decisions.** _**Index Terms**_ **—class imbalance, probability calibration, SMOTE, resampling, tree ensembles, expected calibration error** ## I. INTRODUCTION Class imbalance is pervasive in high-stakes classification: fraud, disease screening, and credit default all present far fewer positive than negative cases. The dominant remedy is _resampling_ — oversampling the minority class (e.g., SMOTE [2]), undersampling the majority, or both. A large literature has grown around these methods [3], and they are routinely judged by how much they improve minority-class recall, F1, or AUC. This evaluation lens is incomplete. Many deployed systems do not consume a hard label; they consume a _predicted probability_ that feeds an expected-cost decision, a triage threshold, or a downstream risk model. For such systems, the quality that matters is _calibration_ : among cases assigned probability _p_ , roughly a fraction _p_ should be positive. Resampling deliberately distorts the class prior the model sees during training, so it is reasonable to suspect it also distorts the probabilities the model emits—yet calibration is rarely measured in imbalanced-learning studies. We ask three questions and answer them empirically on real public data: **(H1)** Does resampling degrade the calibration of tree ensembles? **(H2)** Can a cheap post-hoc recalibration repair the damage without sacrificing ranking power? **(H3)** How does the damage scale with the imbalance ratio? Our contributions are a practical, evidence-based guide rather than a claim of novel discovery. The _phenomenon_ that resampling— specifically undersampling—harms calibration was established by Dal Pozzolo et al. [1]; our value-add is in clarifying _which_ resampling methods demand attention and _which_ repair strategies work: - A unified, multi-seed, paired-statistics comparison that places _synthetic oversampling_ (SMOTE), random over/under-sampling, and a class-weight control on the same footing across five datasets (imbalance ratio 1.9–70) and two tree ensembles, with effect sizes and multiple- comparison correction. - A practical assessment: SMOTE’s calibration penalty is small ( _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27) and its discrimination gains typically justify the cost, whereas random undersampling is genuinely dangerous—its damage grows steeply with imbalance, and on extreme ratios (IR 70) the problem is sample-size collapse rather than resampling _per se_ . - An oversampling-ratio sweep showing the calibration cost grows monotonically with how aggressively the minority class is oversampled. - A _negative result_ (Section VI): the analytic prior-shift correction that repairs undersampling does not transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior—so data-driven posthoc calibration remains necessary. - Concrete recommendations for practitioners: when to worry, when not to, and what to do about it (Section VII-A). ## II. RELATED WORK **Imbalanced learning.** SMOTE [2] introduced synthetic minority interpolation, generating new positive examples along line segments between minority neighbours rather than duplicating them. The idea spawned a large family—borderline, safe-level, and ensemble-integrated variants among them— surveyed in a 15-year retrospective [3]. Random over- and under-sampling remain strong, simple baselines that the same survey reports are often competitive with elaborate synthesis. Across this literature the evaluation protocol is remarkably uniform: methods are ranked by minority-sensitive discrimination scores (F1, G-mean, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC), and the predicted probability is treated as an intermediate quantity to be thresholded, not an output whose quality is assessed in its own right. Our work does not propose a new sampler; it reexamines this entire family through a metric the family has overlooked. **Tree ensembles.** Random forests [4] and gradient boosting [5] are the default models for tabular data, and they calibrate very differently out of the box: bagging tends to produce probabilities pushed away from 0 and 1, while boosting produces sharp, overconfident scores. Because these two mechanisms react differently to a shifted training prior, we study both rather than a single model. **Calibration.** A probabilistic classifier is calibrated when, among instances assigned probability _p_ , a fraction _p_ are positive. Post-hoc methods learn a mapping from raw scores to calibrated probabilities on held-out data: Platt scaling fits a sigmoid, while isotonic regression fits a non-parametric monotone function [6]; beta calibration [7] offers a richer parametric family for binary problems. Miscalibration is not unique to imbalance—modern deep networks are systematically overconfident [8], and the choice of ECE estimator matters: equal-width binning is known to be biased with finite samples, motivating adaptive-binning and KDE-based alternatives [9], [10]. We use equal-width ECE as our primary metric for comparability with prior work but mitigate its limitations through complementary Brier scores and reliability diagrams. The interaction between _deliberate prior distortion via resampling_ and calibration has not been measured systematically at this breadth. That interaction is our subject. **Closest prior work.** Two studies are directly related and we do not claim priority over their core observations. Dal Pozzolo et al. [1] showed that _undersampling_ shifts posterior probabilities and derived a correction for that specific case; Huang et al. [11] experimentally compared calibration techniques on imbalanced data. Bellinger et al. [12] proposed ReMix, a calibrated resampling framework that includes SMOTE variants with integrated calibration; their focus is on method development rather than the systematic measurement and disentanglement of calibration costs that we provide. Our study is complementary rather than novel in its central claim: we extend the lens to _synthetic oversampling_ (SMOTE) and a class-weight control under a unified multi-seed, pairedstatistics protocol, we add an oversampling-ratio sweep, and— most usefully—we report a _negative result_ (Section VI) showing that the analytic prior-shift correction which works for undersampling does _not_ transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density and not merely the prior. We position this paper as a careful consolidation and cautionary extension of these prior findings, not as the discovery of the calibration-degradation phenomenon itself. ## III. METHOD ## _A. Calibration metrics_ Let _p_ ˆ _i_ be the predicted probability of the positive class for test instance _i_ with true label _yi ∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ . We report three complementary measures. **Expected Calibration Error (ECE)** partitions [0 _,_ 1] into _M_ = 10 equal-width bins _Bm_ and computes **==> picture [217 x 29] intentionally omitted <==** where acc( _Bm_ ) is the empirical positive rate and conf( _Bm_ ) the mean predicted probability in bin _m_ . The **Brier score** is the mean squared error _n_[1] � _i_[(ˆ] _[p][i][ −][y][i]_[)][2][.][We][visualise][calibration] with **reliability diagrams** . To confirm that any calibration change is not bought by destroying ranking ability, we also report ROC-AUC and PR-AUC, and—to make the “hidden cost” explicit—minority-class F1. ## _B. Conditions_ Against an unsampled **baseline** , we evaluate three resampling families applied _inside the training fold only_ : SMOTE, random oversampling (ROS), and random undersampling (RUS). To separate “the resampling” from “the model’s behaviour under imbalance,” we add a **class-weight** control that rebalances the loss without altering the data (scikitlearn’s class_weight=’balanced’ for random forest and class_weight=’balanced’ for histogram gradient boosting, which weight samples inversely proportional to class frequency). Finally we apply two **post-hoc recalibrations** to the SMOTE model: Platt scaling (sigmoid) and isotonic regression, each fit on a held-out calibration split disjoint from the model’s training data. ## IV. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP ## _A. Datasets_ We use five real public binary-classification datasets from OpenML, chosen to span a wide imbalance ratio (IR, majority:minority), shown in Table I. Categorical features are onehot encoded and missing values imputed by column medians. The large adult set is stratified-subsampled to 8,000 rows to bound compute. ## _B. Protocol_ Each (dataset, model, condition) cell is evaluated with 5-fold stratified cross-validation repeated over 10 random seeds; resampling and calibration-split selection occur strictly within training folds to prevent leakage. Models are scikit-learn’s random forest (n_estimators=120, max_depth=None, min_samples_split=2, all other defaults) and histogram gradient boosting (max_iter=200, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=None, min_samples_leaf=20, all other defaults). Statistical methodology_ Because metric values across datasets are neither normally distributed nor on a common scale, we avoid parametric tests. For each comparison we form matched pairs over the 5 _×_ 2 _×_ 10 = 100 (dataset, model, seed) tuples— reduced to 50 when a comparison fixes the model—and apply the two-sided _Wilcoxon signed-rank test_ , which assesses whether the median paired difference departs from zero without assuming normality. The two ensembles start from different calibration_ Averaged over conditions, the random forest is far better calibrated than histogram gradient boosting at baseline (ECE 0 _._ 023 vs 0 _._ 082), consistent with the known tendency of boosting to produce sharp, overconfident scores. Crucially, resampling degrades _both_ : SMOTE raises random-forest ECE from 0 _._ 023 to 0 _._ 033 and boosting ECE from 0 _._ 082 to 0 _._ 089. The harm is therefore not an artefact of one model’s idiosyncratic probability behaviour; it appears in the well-calibrated bagging model and the poorly-calibrated boosting model alike, which strengthens the case that the cause is the shifted training prior rather than the estimator. **A learning-curve control confirms these are not convergence artefacts.** Varying _n_ estimators from 5 to 300 trees (RF) and 10 to 500 iterations (HGB) across five seeds and all five datasets, we find: RF calibration _improves_ with more trees (baseline ECE 0 _._ 062 _→_ 0 _._ 024; SMOTE 0 _._ 078 _→_ 0 _._ 035), while HGB calibration _worsens_ (baseline ECE 0 _._ 061 _→_ 0 _._ 125; SMOTE 0 _._ 133 _→_ 0 _._ 121). Validation across all datasets remains future work. ## VI. A NEGATIVE RESULT: PRIOR CORRECTION DOES NOT TRANSFER TO SMOTE For undersampling, the calibration damage is essentially a known prior shift, and Dal Pozzolo et al. [1] exploit this with a closed-form correction. Because SMOTE balances the training prior to _π_ train=0 _._ 5 while deployment faces the true prior _π_ test, the same analytic correction [13], [14] is tempting: it requires _no held-out calibration data_ and, being monotone, cannot harm AUC, **==> picture [225 x 21] intentionally omitted <==** We tested this head-to-head against isotonic recalibration on the SMOTE model (Table V). The result is negative and instructive: the analytic correction does _not_ improve calibration on SMOTE (ECE 0 _._ 090 _→_ 0 _._ 096, Wilcoxon _p_ =0 _._ 80—no significant change), and on adult it is markedly worse (0 _._ 035 _→_ 0 _._ 082). Data-driven isotonic recalibration, by contrast, cuts ECE sharply (0 _._ 090 _→_ 0 _._ 026, _p<_ 10 _[−]_[14] ). As predicted, prior correction leaves AUC untouched (0 _._ 863, identical to uncorrected SMOTE), but that is cold comfort when it fails to fix the calibration it was meant to repair. Our results reframe a routine modelling choice. Resampling is adopted to help the minority class, and by discrimination metrics it appears to do so—F1 is flat or slightly higher. But the same intervention quietly corrupts the predicted probabilities, and the corruption is largest exactly where imbalance is most severe and resampling is most tempting. A practitioner who selects a pipeline on F1 or AUC alone will ship a model whose probabilities are untrustworthy without ever seeing a warning sign. The remedy is cheap and well understood: recalibrate after resampling. One Platt or isotonic step restored calibration to approximately baseline levels (and below baseline in the full 5-dataset aggregate; Table II), and because both are monotone they leave the decision ranking intact. The practical guidance is therefore simple—if you resample and your downstream system consumes probabilities, add a held-out recalibration stage, and prefer SMOTE or oversampling over aggressive undersampling when imbalance is extreme. ## _A. Practical recommendations_ We distil the findings into four concrete guidelines. **(1) Always report calibration.** ECE, Brier, or a reliability diagram should accompany F1/AUC in any imbalanced-learning study; otherwise a real regression in probability quality is invisible. **(2) Recalibrate after resampling** on a held-out split whenever probabilities feed a threshold or expected-cost decision; one isotonic or Platt step suffices and restored ECE to approximately baseline levels (and below baseline in the 5-dataset aggregate) in every case we measured. **(3) Avoid aggressive undersampling at high imbalance.** Its calibration damage is an order of magnitude larger than SMOTE’s and grows with the imbalance ratio; if data volume permits, prefer oversampling or class weighting. **(4) Prefer class weighting when a calibrated probability is the goal and the** **model supports it** , since it barely perturbed calibration (ECE 0 _._ 052 _→_ 0 _._ 058) while still addressing the loss imbalance. ## _B. Reproducibility_ All datasets are public OpenML sets identified by numeric ID (pima: 37, credit-g: 31, phoneme: 1489, adult: 1590, yeast ml8: 316); the preprocessing (one-hot encoding, median imputation, stratified subsampling of adult to 8,000 rows) is deterministic given the seed. Every reported number is the mean over ten seeds and 5-fold stratified cross-validation, with resampling and calibration-split selection confined to training folds. We release the data-fetch script, the experiment runner, the analysis script, and the figure generator, together with the full 700-row results table, so that every figure and statistic can be regenerated end to end. Experiments were run with Python 3.11, scikit-learn 1.6, numpy, pandas, xgboost, lightgbm, and shap; a complete requirements.txt is included in the repository. No GPU is required. ## VIII. LIMITATIONS Our scope is deliberately bounded and our claims should not be overstated. (i) We study two tree ensembles on tabular data; neural models and other modalities may behave differently. (ii) The calibration–AUC trade-off involves both a data-reduction cost ( _∼_ 29% of the total AUC drop; see Section V-C) and a calibration-transform cost; varying the calibration split size would further refine this decomposition. (iii) A learning-curve control (evaluated across 5–300 trees for RF, 10–500 iterations for HGB; Section V-D) confirms RF calibration improves with more trees while HGB calibration worsens, so the reported differences are not underfitting artefacts. (iv) A balanced-downsampling control on yeast_ml8 (§V-D) shows the extreme RUS ECE (0 _._ 395) reflects training on _∼_ 50 samples rather than resampling _per se_ ; RUS remains genuinely damaging on datasets with adequate training size. (v) ECE with four binning strategies (Appendix IX) yields consistent conclusions; equal-width-10 slightly underestimates ECE, so our reported numbers are conservative. (vi) SHAP analysis is limited to credit-g. (vii) Only Platt scaling and isotonic regression are evaluated for post-hoc calibration; other common methods (beta calibration, temperature scaling) may exhibit different trade-offs. (viii) The decomposition of the AUC drop into data-reduction ( _∼_ 29%) and calibrationtransform ( _∼_ 71%) components was measured at a single calibration-split ratio (30%); the fraction attributable to each source likely varies with split size. (ix) The explanation that SMOTE distorts _p_ ( **x** _|y_ ) rather than only _p_ ( _y_ ) is an intuitive hypothesis consistent with our data; a formal analysis of how synthetic interpolation alters the class-conditional density is left for future work. (x) Five tabular datasets and two tree ensembles cannot represent every regime; extensions to neural models, non-tabular modalities, and larger dataset collections are natural next steps. (xi) Our evaluation measures calibration quality (ECE, Brier) and ranking quality (AUC, F1) but stops short of demonstrating the operational cost of miscalibration under a concrete cost matrix; an expectedloss analysis on one or two datasets would strengthen the practical argument. (xii) A synthetic 2D example illustrating how SMOTE interpolation distorts _p_ ( **x** _|y_ =1) would make the mechanistic explanation in Section VI more concrete. The SMOTE cost findings are bounded by the tested imbalance range (IR 1.9–70); imbalances of hundreds or thousands may produce larger effects. The SMOTE results use a fixed number of neighbours ( _k_ =5); varying _k_ may alter the geometry of synthetic samples and the resulting calibration cost. The AUCdrop decomposition is reported as a point estimate; bootstrap confidence intervals would quantify its stability. ## IX. CONCLUSION Resampling for class imbalance can degrade probability calibration, but the severity depends on the method and the imbalance ratio: SMOTE’s cost is real but small across the studied range (IR 1.9–70; _δ_ = + 0 _._ 27) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the penalty; random undersampling is the genuine danger, especially at high imbalance ratios, where the problem is as much about sample-size collapse as about resampling _per se_ . A single post-hoc recalibration step repairs either case at negligible ranking cost (AUC _−_ 0 _._ 002, Cliff’s _δ_ = _−_ 0 _._ 07). The analytic prior-shift correction that works for undersampling does not transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior—so data-driven recalibration remains the only reliable repair. We urge that imbalanced-learning evaluations report calibration alongside F1 and AUC, and that probabilityconsuming systems recalibrate after any resampling. ## APPENDIX: ECE ESTIMATOR ROBUSTNESS The primary results use equal-width ECE with 10 bins. To verify conclusions are not an artefact of this estimator, we recomputed ECE with four variants on two datasets (pima, credit_g, RF, 10 seeds): equal-width-10, equal-width-15, adaptive-binning (equal mass per bin), and ECEsweep (mean over 5–30 bins; Roelofs et al.). All agree in sign and rank order (Table VI): RUS _≫_ SMOTE _>_ baseline _>_ calibrated. Equal-width-10 slightly _underestimates_ ECE relative to adaptive/sweep, so our reported numbers are conservative.
